Summary: In colorectal cancer, KLF4 functions context‑dependently: it acts as a tumor suppressor in early‑stage disease by inducing apoptosis and inactivating the WNT pathway. KLF4 acts as an oncogene in late‑stage disease by inducing stemness. Fusobacterium nucleatum increased intracellular Ca2+ concentration and promoted an interaction between E‑cadherin and KLF4. That interaction led to KLF4 phosphorylation and nuclear translocation. Nuclear KLF4 induced ITGA5 transcription and activated downstream signaling. The E‑cadherin/KLF4/ITGA5 signaling promoted CRC growth and metastasis. KLF4 protein increased via post‑transcriptional regulation without a rise in KLF4 mRNA. PRMT5 interacts with KLF4 and induces methylation of arginine 374, 376, and 377 in KLF4, contributing to KLF4 transcriptional activity. p300/CBP acetylation at lysine 225 or 229 inhibited KLF4 activation of downstream targets. KLF4 knockdown reversed F. nucleatum–induced upregulation of integrin α5 protein and reduced ITGA5 mRNA. In primary human CRC associated with F. nucleatum, KLF4 protein and ITGA5 mRNA were higher than in CRC unrelated to F. nucleatum, and across patients ITGA5 mRNA correlated positively with KLF4 protein. In the context of Fusobacterium nucleatum abundance, ITGA5 and KLF4 levels correlated with TNM stage. Knocking down KLF4 or ITGA5 antagonized F. nucleatum’s oncogenic effects in patient‑derived organoids, subcutaneous xenografts, orthotopic tumors, and liver metastasis in nude mice. Chelating Ca2+ antagonized F. nucleatum's oncogenic role. Knocking down E‑cadherin antagonized the impact of F. nucleatum on KLF4.
Reference: Yan, X., Qu, X., Wang, J., Lu, L., Wu, W., Mao, J., Li, D., Wang, Y., Wei, Q., & Liu, J. (2025). <i>Fusobacterium nucleatum</i> Promotes the Growth and Metastasis of Colorectal Cancer by Activating E-Cadherin/Krüppel-Like Factor 4/Integrin α5 Signaling in a Calcium-Dependent Manner. MedComm (2020), 6(3), e70137. https://doi.org/10.1002/mco2.70137

Summary: In colorectal cancer, KLF4 functions as an upstream regulator of COL12A1. ChIP assays confirmed KLF4 enrichment at the COL12A1 promoter, and combined with JASPAR predictions, dual-luciferase reporter assays showed KLF4 overexpression reduced COL12A1-WT reporter activity but not MUT1/3, supporting that KLF4 binds at binding site 2. One experimental series reports that KLF4 facilitates COL12A1 transcription. That series hypothesizes that increased COL12A1 promotes ERK1/2 phosphorylation and nuclear translocation and may activate transcription factors that regulate IDO1. However, KLF4 overexpression in HCT116 cells reduced COL12A1 expression. Pearson correlation analysis showed a positive correlation between KLF4 and COL12A1 expression. KLF4 upregulation is generally associated with glycolysis suppression in colorectal cancer. Caffeine treatment consistently upregulated KLF4 across CRC cell lines, reduced aerobic glycolysis in HCT116 CRC cells, and decreased COL12A1 expression. The mechanism by which caffeine regulates KLF4 and the reported bidirectional correlation between KLF4 and glycolysis remain unclear.
Reference: Liu, Y., Liu, Z., Hu, Y., Ling, Y., Qing, S., Liu, Y., Zhan, Y., Shen, Z., Fang, Y., & Deng, H. (2025). Caffeine enhances antitumor T-cell activity by suppressing kynurenine pathway in colorectal cancer. Nat Commun, 16(1), 5906. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-60958-0

Summary: KLF4 dampens pre-metastatic niche formation and metastasis in colorectal cancer. Colorectal cancer–secreted exosomal miR-25-3p is present in vascular endothelial cells (shown in HUVECs) and targets and downregulates KLF4. KLF4 downregulation correlates with decreased KLF2 and decreased tight-junction proteins ZO-1, occludin and Claudin5 in lung and liver, and with increased VEGFR2 in lung and liver. Colorectal cancer–secreted miR-25-3p promotes angiogenesis and destroys endothelial tight junctions in the lung and liver, increasing vascular permeability and pre-metastatic niche formation. In mice pretreated with colorectal cancer–derived miR-25-3p exosomes, endothelial barrier destruction coincided with increased metastatic burden in the lung and liver.
Reference: Zeng, Z., Li, Y., Pan, Y., Lan, X., Song, F., Sun, J., Zhou, K., Liu, X., Ren, X., Wang, F., Hu, J., Zhu, X., Yang, W., Liao, W., Li, G., Ding, Y., & Liang, L. (2018). Cancer-derived exosomal miR-25-3p promotes pre-metastatic niche formation by inducing vascular permeability and angiogenesis. Nat Commun, 9(1), 5395. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-07810-w

Summary: In human colorectal tumors, KLF4 is significantly downregulated relative to peritumor tissues. TCGA data confirm a negative correlation between KLF4 and MEX3A. Low KLF4 expression associates with poor survival. E2F3 induces MEX3A, and MEX3A directly suppresses KLF4. KLF4 functions as a terminal differentiation regulator and tumor suppressor in CRC. KLF4 inhibits WNT signaling by competitively interacting with β‑catenin in HCT116 colon cancer cells. Suppression of KLF4 activates WNT signaling to sustain CRC cells in a rapidly dividing and undifferentiated state. MEX3A activity helps retain cancer stem cells and intestinal stem cells in an undifferentiated and rapidly-dividing state. BMP4 treatment of 3D Caco‑2 colon cancer cultures upregulates KLF4. Pharmacologic activation of KLF4 with APTO‑253 suppresses colon tumor organoid growth by promoting differentiation. Thus the MEX3A–KLF4 axis coordinates WNT and BMP signaling to help balance self‑renewal and differentiation in ISCs and cancer stem cells.
Reference: Yang, X., Li, G., Tian, Y., Wang, X., Xu, J., Liu, R., Deng, M., Shao, C., Pan, Y., Wu, X., Li, M., Zhang, C., Liu, R., Qin, J., Zhang, C., Liu, Z., Wu, X., Plikus, M. V., Lengner, C. J., Zheng, Z., Lv, C., & Yu, Z. (2022). Identifying the E2F3-MEX3A-KLF4 signaling axis that sustains cancer cells in undifferentiated and proliferative state. Theranostics, 12(16), 6865-6882. https://doi.org/10.7150/thno.76619

Summary: KLF4 functions as a tumor suppressor in colorectal cancer. KLF4 is downregulated in human colorectal cancer tissues. KLF4 inhibits colorectal cancer growth in mice. KLF4 inhibits colorectal cancer cell proliferation by upregulating p21 and NDRG2 and by downregulating cyclinD1, GINS4, and IFITM3. KLF4 deficiency accelerates colitis and colon cancer progression. KLF4 deficiency is associated with tumor development via epithelial–mesenchymal transition. Multiple microRNAs, including microRNA-92a and microRNA-10b, downregulate KLF4 and thereby promote colorectal cancer growth, migration, proliferation, and metastasis. Compounds have targeted KLF4. APTO-253, a KLF4 inducer, halted progression of some advanced tumors, especially leukemia, in a clinical trial. Development was discontinued after the company reported MYC inhibition. In colon cells CDX2 upregulated KLF4 by binding the KLF4 promoter and reducing association of histone 3 lysine 4 trimethylation with the KDM5B demethylase. CDX2 acted as a tumor suppressor. In a clinical trial metformin increased KLF4 expression and had a positive effect on colorectal cancer and endometrial cancer. Transcription factors are generally considered undruggable. It is premature to consider KLF4 as a general target for cancer therapy.
Reference: He, Z., He, J., & Xie, K. (2023). KLF4 transcription factor in tumorigenesis. Cell Death Discov, 9(1), 118. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41420-023-01416-y
